title: Should the Fed Look Through the Latest Inflationary Supply Shock?

Key indicators to track for our sticky
inflation theme are core PCE and super
core PCE, which are both in strong
positive impulses at 4.4% through month
annualized and 4.5% through month
annualized respectively.
>> The April headline CPI, food CPI, and
energy CPI data supported our sticky
inflation theme. April headline CPI is
in a strong positive impulse up 7.1%
through month annualized. Food CPI
strong positive impulse up 3.6% through
month annualized. Energy CPI up 63%
through month annualized strong positive
impulse as well. The April core CPI,
core goods CPI, core services CPI, and
super core CPI data supported our sticky
inflation theme. We got a weak positive
impulse in core CPI up 3.2% through
month annualized. Weak positive impulse
in core goods CPI up 0.9%. We got a
strong positive impulse in core services
CPI up 4% and then we got a a weak
positive impulse in super core CPI up
3.9%.
All these numbers are comfortably above
their trends. The April shelter CPI data
supported our sticky inflation theme and
did not support our cooling housing and
labor theme. So shelter CPI, they
basically corrected the
the lapse of of of of reporting from
October and so there was some giveback
there that created a strong positive
impulse in the shelter CPI data up 4.4%.
We don't think that's durable. We
continue to see the leading indicator of
of of of of you know shelter CPI and
housing PCE, which is rents nationwide
rents. You know we got a strong negative
impulse in nationwide rents at the
lowest level we've seen since going back
to
kind of the early days of COVID. So
ultimately these are going to resolve.
That's going to resolve itself
positively in the coming quarters and
this will kind of you know break back
down to a down trend as well. But for
now not not moving in the right
direction. The April median CPI data
supported our sticky inflation theme. So
median CPI we got a weak positive
impulse there up 3.1% through month
annualized breaking back out above
trend. The April trim mean CPI data
supported our sticky inflation theme. We
got a strong positive impulse in trim
mean CPI up 3.4% on a three month
annualized basis. That number is well
above trend uh and so this is a problem
for Kevin Warsh. Recall that he was
trying to pivot uh the Fed's reaction
function to uh trim mean inflation
statistics to kind of, you know, give uh
the give the you know, create more
create the conditions for uh more
near-term policy rate easing, but in our
opinion, uh
easing policy at any point in time
probably between now and year end will
cause a meltdown in the bond market. Uh
you know, uh for those it may sound
counterintuitive, but for those who may
be unfamiliar with these income
dynamics, easy money is reflationary and
inflationary, particularly when you
already have an inflation problem, and
so that causes investors to uh shun uh
duration risk. Uh conversely, tight
money causes investors to want to buy uh
bonds because ultimately you're slowing
down, uh you know, the kind of nominal
growth in the economy. And so, uh you
know, in our opinion, we think the
probability of of of of tight money uh
coming, uh you know, the it it it it
will ultimately kind of calm down some
volatility in the bond market, but the
pivot from
pricing in easy money to now pricing in
tight money, that that that period of
time is the issue. Once you get over uh
the once the Fed gets above, you know,
out in front of the curve, that's when
you can start to, you know, buy duration
and and expect that the Fed has your
back if you're going to be investing in
the long bond, but right now, the market
is is not clear whether the Fed has
their back uh for for now, and then
we'll talk about why uh that may be the
case. And ultimately, to the extent that
they have to pivot to having the bond
market's back, it's going to come at the
expense of the equity market. Uh the
April headline PPI, core PPI, and core
PPI ex-trade services, uh goods PPI, and
services PPI data supported our sticky
inflation theme as well, so we got a
strong positive impulse in headline PPI
up 10.7% through month annualized. Weak
positive impulse in core PPI uh up up
6.5% through month annualized. We got a
weak positive impulse in core PPI
ex-trade services up 5.2% through month
annualized. We got a strong positive
impulse in goods PPI up 19.9% through
month annualized. And then we got a uh
strong positive impulse in services PPI
up 7.1% through month annualized. All
these numbers are well, well, well above
uh trend. Uh as we predicted over 2
years ago, the structurally elevated
bottom in the core PPI cycle perfectly
presaged similar dynamics in core CPI
and core PCE, strongly supporting our
sticky inflation theme uh in the
process. So, again, we were very clear
back in, you know, early 2024, we were
like, "Hey,
we're bottoming at a very high level in
core core PPI. Uh, it's been very
correlated with core CPI, core PCE,
we're so we're probably going to bottom
in uh higher levels uh um, you know, in
in those indices, indices, but again,
don't forget you have shelter and
housing, you know, kind of the roll
through the the lagging impact of that
on those statistics, you know, allowed
those part that made those processes
take a little bit longer, but
ultimately, the you know, our conclusion
was correct, which is we're going to
bottom at a structurally elevated level
in both of those statistics, and and
that's exactly what we're seeing. We're
now starting to accelerate. Uh, we've
been accelerating in core PCE, and we're
now accelerating uh in core CPI as well.
Uh, in fact, the 42 macro cycle and
inflation model has accurately
forecasted an equilibrium core PCE rate
in the high twos uh low uh threes since
January of 2022, when we authored uh the
sticky inflation theme. Recall it when
we authored the sticky inflation theme,
uh the consensus at the time was
inflation was transitory. That was
wrong. Uh, when we authored our
resilient US economy theme in the summer
of 2022, the consensus at the time was
US is headed into a recession. That was
wrong. Uh, when we authored our paradigm
shift theme, uh aka Reddit hot theme in
April of 2025, the consensus at the time
was tariffs are going to tank the US and
global economy. That was wrong. So, uh
you know, we know what we're doing here
in terms of, you know, fading uh Wall
Street consensus on on some of these
fundamental themes, and you know, this
has been as right as rain, and we're
doing you know, it's
4-plus years later, we're still talking
about the same exact uh information, the
same exact chart, the same exact uh
theme, and and so it it's obviously not
going away uh unless the Fed really
wants to get out in front of it uh
and really, you know, do what it has to
do from a business cycle standpoint uh
to to to to to to to to to rinse out all
this, you know, kind of legacy inflation
pressure. And and that's exactly what
the model's been signaling uh for over 4
years now. Uh reminder, inflation is by
far the most lagging indicator of the
business cycle and is unlikely to
durably return to the Fed's arbitrary 2%
target without an actual recession. Uh
so again, if you look at the business
cycle, we know that there is a, you
know, kind of a you know, a
rough sequence to how the business cycle
process works, particularly when you're
heading into a downturn or recession. So
policy tends to break uh down or, you
know, kind of get tight, let's call it
about, you know, 12 to 15 18 months
ahead of recession, we'll call it 18
months ahead of recession, the corporate
profits break down about a year ahead of
recession, then liquidity breaks down
about three quarters ahead of recession,
then growth in stocks break down
simultaneously about two quarters ahead
of recession, then credit breaks down,
and employment breaks down
simultaneously right when the recession
starts, and then finally, inflation
breaks down about a year uh after, you
know, 12 to 15 12 to 15 months, you
know, four to five quarters after a
recession starts.
So unless you go into recession and stay
there for a while, it's unlikely that
the Fed is going to uh have inflation
return durably to its arbitrary 2%
target. Uh so we know that, we you know,
we've been saying this for years that
the Fed doesn't want 2% inflation. The
Fed's pretending like it wants 2%
inflation to maintain stability in the
long end of the curve and
and anchor long-term inflation
expectations, uh but the reality is is
is that frog slide that is at the end of
every 42 macro research report. We're
all frogs being boiled alive in a pot of
monetary debasement and financial
oppression. This is why our KISS model
portfolio does not own bonds.
This is why instead of playing defense
with bonds, which are no longer
defensive of assets for a variety of
reasons, not the least of which is
they're now positively correlated to
stocks, instead of playing defense with
bonds, we play defense with ball
targeting and dynamic position size.
That in my opinion is a much better
solution, particularly uh as these
dynamics continue to linger. Uh the 42
macro business cycle model currently
signals a low probability of a
development recession in the US economy.
So if you, you know, going back to
how the business cycle process works, we
can just look at the current data and
say there's no, you know, we have no we
have none of this going on right now in
the current data. If anything, we're
starting to hook up uh uh from a
business cycle standpoint. So, there's a
low probability of a developing
recession in US economy. So, we don't
have to be concerned about the Fed
pivoting hot The Fed should not be
concerned about pivoting pivoting
hawkishly from the perspective of what
what it what is it going to do to growth
and what is it going to do to the
business cycle. In fact, initial and
continuing jobless claims currently
signal a low probability of a developing
recession in US economy. The three-month
annualized rates of change on the
four-week moving average of initial
claims and continuing claims are well
below the recession signaling
thresholds. The 42 macro five recession
signaling indicators currently signal a
low probability of a developing
recession in the resilient US economy.
If you look at special considerations
out of the US, so obviously the US is
the dominant economy in the world. It's
the what the US economy does is
generally going to dictate the market
regime. It's going to have the biggest
influence over
the market regime. So, we've looked at a
few special special considerations for
the US. So, we'll start with US
inflation policy drivers, the policy
drivers of US inflation. And so, we're
looking at this from the perspective of
fiscal policy. Looking at this from the
perspective of monetary policy, looking
at this from the perspective of
regulatory policy, particularly as it
relates to the credit cycle. So,
starting with the top panel here, we got
total accumulated US federal budget
deficits, which other people would just
call total debt.
>> [laughter]
>> That 30.7 trillion dollars. We have a
We got below trend growth there at 4.4%
three-month annualized. So, below trend
growth of accumulated federal budget
deficits signals little need for the Fed
to respond with rate hikes. But we also
have well above trend growth of Fed
public debt monetization, which is the
Fed's, you know, exposure to the
Treasury market in their portfolio. And
we have well above trend growth of US
commercial bank loans and leases. And
so, both of these dynamics at 12.6%
three-month annualized and 9.5%
three-month annualized respectively,
these growth rates signal the Fed would
be wise to tighten in respond to these
upside risks to inflation. Again, the
Fed is monetizing US government debt at
a three-month annualized rate of 12.6%
and the commercial bank loans and leases
US commercial banks are extending credit
at a three-month annualized rate of
almost 10% which is basically double the
long
pre-COVID trend rate and obviously in a
strong positive impulse there signals
that hey you know that the bank credit
cycle which is exactly what we predicted
when we offered the paradigm C run it
hot theme back in April of last year we
said hey one of the things that is going
to be very under appreciated is the
deregulation particularly the
deregulation in the financial sector
which is ultimately cause a pretty
meaningful upside risk in the credit
cycle here in the US and this is exactly
what we're seeing here so in the context
of a Fed that is monetizing debt and a
meaningful strong positive impulse in
the credit cycle here in the US the Fed
has to take this into consideration when
it decides it wants to quote unquote let
the markets do the work for them. Hey
you guys aren't being paid hundreds of
thousands of dollars a year to let
markets do the work for you. Do your
job.
We have a nationwide affordability
crisis that they were roughly 60%
responsible for because they thought the
markets were going to do the work for
them back in 2020 and 2021 but I
digress.
If you look at the monetary drivers of
US inflation so we have well above trend
growth
in the monetary basis is obviously you
know a lot of this is from the Fed's
reserve management purchase program at
5.6.3%
through with annualized it suggests the
Fed should truncate our reserve
management purchases. The repo market is
healed we talked about that last week
the repo market is fine it's as good as
it's been since prior to the issues that
we started highlighting in the repo
market any early fall of last year. And
so the repo market is fine so there's no
reason for the Fed to be annualizing the
purchases of of monetization of US
government debt to the tune of about 650
billion dollars. There's no reason for
the Fed to be doing that in the context
of having headline inflation at 7.1%
through with annualized core PC
inflation at 4.4% 4% through with
annualized trim mean CPI at 3.4% through
with annualized numbers are all way too
high relative to the Fed's you know
arbitrary 2% a
Uh
If you look at US money supply, M1 money
supply tracking right at trend at 7.2%
through my angle lines, M2 money supply
tracking right at trend at 5.9% through
my angle lines. So, you know, that's
okay. It doesn't It means the Fed can
perhaps, you know, wait and not be too
concerned. But what is concerning is the
context that money money velocity. We
have just a higher velocity economy both
in respect of M1 money supply. If you
look at the ratio between M1 and M0,
that's a 3.6 relative to a pre-COVID
trend of 0.9. And then M2 money
velocity, if you look at the ratio of M2
to M0 at 4.2, that is way higher than
the pre-COVID trend of 3.7. So, you just
have a higher velocity of money economy.
We're going
above trend from the standpoint of
base money. We're going trend We're
going at trend from the standpoint of
narrow money and going at trend from the
standpoint of broad money. And so,
the risk is is that the pullback that we
currently see in cumulative federal
budget deficit actually starts to widen
again, which is exactly what we're
expecting in the next 6 months. Uh we
talked about that a couple of weeks ago.
And so, if we get the fiscal thrust
starting to to ramp back up again in the
context of the Fed monetizing debt in
the context of a explosive growth in
commercial bank loans and leases and in
the context of, you know, structurally
elevated money velocity, this is how you
can get another inflation accident. And
so, you know, wrapping up,
the probability of the Fed contributing
to another inflation accident
is rising according to the market's
estimates of the neutral rate, which is
currently 50 basis points
above the Fed funds rate, the effective
Fed funds rate, and the market's
estimate of R-star, which is currently
75 basis points above the
Holston-Laubach-Williams model estimate.
A Fed that does not act to take away the
punch bowl will fan the flames of an
overheating economy and stock market
bubble. Again, the market is now saying
the Fed needs to hike twice
to remove its accommodative bias. The
Fed is now accommodating nominal GDP
growth at a time where every meaningful
if statistic of core inflation or
underlying inflation is either in a
strong positive impulse or a weak
positive impulse at a level at a 3-month
annualized rate that is wickedly
inconsistent with the Fed's 2% target.
And so there's this, you know, lazy
thought process out there. I keep
hearing about it, which is the Fed
should look through
supply shocks. They got to They all
these central banks got to look through
supply shocks. Well, tell that to the
people who
can't trade down anymore at the grocery
store. They just now have to do without.
We have a 5-year long nationwide
affordability crisis
that is only getting worse. Obviously,
we got 90 nation gas prices in five, you
know, heading probably heading to $6
this summer. Uh you know, this is this
is You know, the Fed's got to make a
decision here.
Does it
care more about the stock market
than it does about the people
with whom it's entrusted to
maintain sound monetary policy for?
I mean, I think we know the answer to
that. If anybody's read The Creature
from Jekyll Island, we all think we I
think we know the answer to that. And

congratulations. I wish you well. I hope

prevents and and and and and and and has
the gumption, the the courage required
to sacrifice the stock market

the the well-being of the folks at the
